preFix.title: Hanging a door with rising butt hinges
preFix.subtitle: Fit rising butt hinges so the door clears a carpet or threshold strip when opening
preFix.excerpt: How to fit a pair of rising butt hinges to an internal door and frame so the door rises as it opens and clears a fitted carpet or threshold strip, including marking and cutting the hinge recesses, setting the correct gap, and adjusting for a door that binds at the top corner.
preFix.sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Ironmongery World hinge installation notes. Architectural Ironmongery specification guide.

content.text: solves the problem of a door that drags across carpet as it opens. The hinge is handed: left-handed for a door that opens to the left when viewed from the opening face, right-handed for the opposite: so confirm the hand before ordering. Note that rising butt hinges require a
on the leading edge of the door; skip this step and the door will bind at the top when it opens past 30°.

Mark the top hinge position 150 mm from the top of the door and the bottom hinge position 225 mm from the bottom. These are standard positions for internal doors. Hold each hinge against the edge of the door with the knuckle projecting beyond the face, and mark around the hinge flap with a sharp pencil. Transfer the same positions to the door frame jamb, checking that the knuckle of each hinge will be in the correct position (the knuckle of a rising butt hinge must project just clear of the frame face).

deeply with the chisel bevel-down to define the boundary. Make a series of cross-grain cuts inside the recess to the flap depth: approximately 2 mm: and pare out the waste with the chisel held flat. Test the hinge flap in the recess: it should sit perfectly flush with no rocking. Deepen any points where the flap stands proud and pare cleanly.
Step 3, apply the top corner relief

Before fitting the door, plane a 2 to 3 mm chamfer on the top leading corner of the door edge: the corner that would meet the frame head as the door rises and opens. Use the block plane set finely and work from the corner outward, checking the angle with the combination square. This chamfer allows the rising door to clear the frame head when fully open.
Step 4, fix the hinges and hang the door

Screw the frame-side hinge flaps into the jamb recesses using the supplied screws. Fit the door-side flaps to the door. Hang the door by placing the door hinge knuckle over the frame hinge knuckle: rising butt hinges separate at the knuckle and rejoin by lowering the door onto the frame-fixed pin. Ensure the knuckles are fully engaged before releasing the door.

Open the door slowly and watch the top leading corner. It should clear the frame head by 2 to 3 mm at full rise. Check the bottom clearance over the carpet: the minimum clearance is 5 mm to prevent the door dragging on heavy carpet pile. Check the door closes flush with the frame face on both sides. If the door binds on the latch side when closing, the hinges are sitting slightly proud: deepen the recesses fractionally.

Rising butt hinges are sold in bright steel, satin nickel, and brass finishes. They are not suitable for heavy fire doors: rising butts are rated for maximum door weights of approximately 40 kg. For heavier doors or fire-rated doorsets, a separate adjustable floor spring or a rising hinge mechanism built into the floor is used.

attrs.fix: Remove the door, enlarge the chamfer on the top leading corner with the block plane, and rehang. Check the rise value stamped on the hinge — 8 mm is standard; plan the chamfer depth to clear by at least 2 mm.
attrs.cause: Top corner relief not cut; or relief too small for the rise of the hinge.
attrs.symptom: Door binds against the frame head when opened past 30°

attrs.fix: Check that the hinge helix rises in the correct direction — the knuckle should lift the door as it opens, which means the hinge is handed. An upside-down rising butt hinge will lower the door as it opens.
attrs.cause: Door not heavy enough for the hinge rise; hinge fitted upside down.
attrs.symptom: Door does not close on its own despite rising butt hinges

attrs.fix: Lift the door fully onto the pin by raising the door during assembly. If the pin is worn, the hinge should be replaced.
attrs.cause: The two hinge halves not fully engaged on the pin; hinge pin worn or bent.
attrs.symptom: Hinge knuckle catches and rattles when the door is opened and closed

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
